content:
# Joshua Chapter 11 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 235-236 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_011_page_001.jpg`

---

*[The Northern Campaign — Twenty-three verses of total war across the entire land. When King Jabin of Hazor hears what Joshua has done in the south, he assembles the largest coalition yet: every king of the north, with armies as countless as sand on the seashore, horses and chariots beyond number. YHWH says: Do not fear them. Joshua strikes by surprise at the waters of Merom, and the rout extends from the Galilee to the coast of Sidon. Hazor, the greatest city in Canaan, burns. The chapter then pulls back to panoramic view — the whole land, north to south, falls. Only one city made peace. Only three cities still harbor giants. And then the summary line that closes an era: the land rested from war. PNEUMA renders the narrative with its gathering momentum, the theological explanations embedded without apology, and the quiet finality of that closing sentence.]*

---

**1** When Jabin king of Hazor heard what had happened, he sent word to Jobab king of Madon, to the king of Shimron, to the king of Achshaph, **2** and to the kings of the north — those in the hill country, those in the Jordan Valley south of Chinneroth, those in the lowlands, and those on the coastal heights of Dor to the west — **3** the Canaanites to the east and west, the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, and Jebusites in the hill country, and the Hivites below Hermon in the region of Mizpah.

**4** They marched out with all their forces — a fighting force as vast as the sand on the seashore, with horses and chariots beyond counting. **5** All these kings joined forces and came together, setting up camp at the waters of Merom to fight against Israel.

**6** YHWH said to Joshua, "Do not be afraid of them. By this time tomorrow I will hand all of them over to Israel, slain. You are to hamstring their horses and burn their chariots."

**7** Joshua and his entire fighting force came upon them suddenly at the waters of Merom and fell on them. **8** YHWH gave them into Israel's hand. They struck them down and chased them as far as Great Sidon, as far as Misrephoth-maim, and as far as the Mizpeh Valley to the east — striking them until no survivor remained. **9** Joshua did exactly what YHWH had told him: he hamstrung their horses and burned their chariots.

**10** At that time Joshua turned back and captured Hazor, and struck down its king with the sword. Hazor had been the leading power among all these kingdoms. **11** They put every person in it to the sword, devoting them to destruction under the ban. Nothing that breathed was left alive. Then he burned Hazor to the ground.

**12** Joshua captured all the cities of these kings and all their kings. He put them to the sword and devoted them to destruction, just as Moses the servant of YHWH had commanded. **13** But the cities built on their ancient mounds — Israel did not burn any of those, except Hazor alone. That one Joshua burned.

**14** All the plunder of these cities, including the livestock, the Sons of Israel took for themselves. But every human being they struck down with the sword until they had wiped them out. They did not leave anything that breathed. **15** What YHWH had commanded Moses his servant, Moses commanded Joshua, and Joshua carried it out. He did not deviate from a single word of all that YHWH had commanded Moses.

**16** So Joshua took the entire land: the hill country, the whole Negev, all the land of Goshen, the lowlands, the Jordan Valley, and the hill country of Israel with its foothills — **17** from Mount Halak, which rises toward Seir, all the way to Baal-gad in the Lebanon Valley below Mount Hermon. He captured all their kings, struck them down, and put them to death.

**18** Joshua waged war against all these kings for a long time. **19** Not a single city made peace with the Sons of Israel — except the Hivites who lived in Gibeon. Every other city they took by force of arms. **20** For it was YHWH's doing that their hearts were hardened to meet Israel in battle, so that they would be devoted to total destruction and receive no mercy, but be wiped out — just as YHWH had commanded Moses.

**21** At that time Joshua went and cut down the Anakim from the hill country — from Hebron, from Debir, from Anab, from all the hill country of Judah, and from all the hill country of Israel. Joshua devoted them and their cities to destruction. **22** No Anakim were left in the land of the Sons of Israel. Only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod did any survive.

**23** So Joshua took the whole land, in keeping with everything YHWH had spoken to Moses. Joshua gave it to Israel as their inheritance, divided among the tribes.

And the land rested from war.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1 — "Jabin" (Yavin):** The name means "he perceives" or "he discerns" — and what he perceives is that the southern campaign has succeeded. His response is not surrender but the assembly of the largest military coalition in the book. A "Jabin king of Hazor" also appears in Judges 4:2, likely a dynastic title rather than the same individual. Hazor was the dominant city-state of northern Canaan, the largest fortified city in the region (roughly 200 acres at its peak), attested in the Mari archives and the Amarna letters. When Hazor moved, everyone moved.

**V. 2 — "The Jordan Valley south of Chinneroth":** The Hebrew reads "the Arabah south of Kinneroth" — the Arabah being the rift valley, and Kinneroth being the region around what we know as the Sea of Galilee. The name Kinneroth comes from kinnor ("lyre"), perhaps describing the lake's shape. PNEUMA renders "Jordan Valley" for the Arabah here because the dynamic principle favors the recognizable geographical term. "The heights of Dor" (naphoth Dor) refers to the coastal ridgeline near Mount Carmel.

**V. 3 — "The Hivites below Hermon in the region of Mizpah":** These Hivites are distinct from the Gibeonites (also Hivites) who made peace with Israel in chapter 9. The land of Mizpah ("watchtower") refers to the Beqa valley at the foot of Hermon. The coalition thus spans from the far north to the coastal plain — a comprehensive northern alliance.

**V. 4 — "As vast as the sand on the seashore":** The Hebrew reads "as the sand that is upon the lip of the sea" — using the word saphah ("lip/edge") for the seashore. This is the standard biblical hyperbole for uncountable numbers, and it deliberately echoes the Abrahamic promise of Genesis 22:17. The irony is sharp: the same language used for the future multitude of Abraham's descendants is here applied to the enemy army that will be destroyed to make room for those descendants.

**V. 6 — "You are to hamstring their horses and burn their chariots":** The verb 'iqqer means to sever the hamstring tendons of the hind legs, permanently crippling the animals. This is not cruelty for its own sake — it is the deliberate destruction of military technology. YHWH commands Israel to neutralize the chariotry rather than capture it. The theological logic follows Deuteronomy 17:16: Israel must not accumulate horses. Victory comes from YHWH, not from superior armament. PNEUMA renders the meaning plainly.

**V. 8 — "Great Sidon... Misrephoth-maim... the Mizpeh Valley":** The pursuit extends in three directions — northwest to the Phoenician coast (Sidon, modern Saida in Lebanon), west-southwest to Misrephoth-maim (literally "burnings of waters," probably a site with hot springs or lime kilns near the coast), and east to the Mizpeh Valley. The rout was total: the coalition army scattered in every direction and was hunted down everywhere.

**V. 10 — "Hazor had been the leading power":** The Hebrew says Hazor "formerly was the head (rosh) of all these kingdoms." The word "formerly" (lefanim) indicates its established pre-eminence. Archaeologically, Hazor's destruction layer from this period shows intense burning — one of the clearest correlations between the biblical text and the material record.

**V. 13 — "Cities built on their ancient mounds":** The Hebrew reads "cities standing upon their tel" — the tel (tell) being the artificial hill formed by successive layers of occupation and destruction over centuries. PNEUMA renders "ancient mounds" to convey both the physical reality and the time-depth. The note is practically significant: Israel burned only Hazor. The other conquered cities were left structurally intact, presumably for later Israelite use. This detail also explains the archaeological puzzle of why most Canaanite city-mounds do not show Late Bronze Age destruction layers.

**V. 15 — The chain of command:** "YHWH commanded Moses... Moses commanded Joshua... Joshua carried it out." This triple link is the narrator's theological summary of the entire conquest: perfect obedience flowing down a chain of authority. The Hebrew emphasizes it with the verb tsivvah ("commanded") appearing three times, and the closing phrase "he did not turn aside a word" (lo hesir davar). For the narrator, the northern campaign's success is not primarily military — it is the result of unbroken obedience to divine instruction.

**V. 20 — "It was YHWH's doing that their hearts were hardened":** This is the most theologically loaded verse in the chapter. The Hebrew lechazzeq et-libbam ("to make strong their heart") uses the same verb (chazaq) used for the hardening of Pharaoh's heart in Exodus. The narrator explains that the Canaanites' refusal to make peace (as the Gibeonites did) was not merely their own political choice — it was divinely orchestrated so that the cherem (total destruction) could be carried out without compromise. The word chinnah ("grace/mercy") appears in the negative: "so that there would be no mercy for them." PNEUMA renders this theological explanation directly. It is one of the most difficult passages in the Hebrew Bible, and PNEUMA presents it without softening or added commentary, letting it stand as the text stands.

**V. 21 — "The Anakim":** The giant clan whose fearsome reputation caused Israel's failure of nerve at Kadesh-Barnea a generation earlier (Numbers 13:28, 33). The spies' report — "we seemed like grasshoppers" — led to forty years of wilderness wandering. Now Joshua systematically eliminates the very people Israel once feared too much to face. The narrative arc from Numbers 13 to Joshua 11 is complete: what the fathers could not do because of fear, the sons have done in obedience.

**V. 22 — "Only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod":** Three cities of the Philistine Pentapolis. This small note — tucked into the end of the conquest summary — is a seed that will sprout generations later. The giants who survived in Gath include the ancestors of Goliath (1 Samuel 17). The text is honest: the conquest was comprehensive but not absolute. Pockets of the old world survived in Philistine territory.

**V. 23 — "And the land rested from war":** The Hebrew shaqetah (from shaqat, "to be quiet, to settle") denotes not merely the absence of fighting but a state of deep repose — the stillness that follows completed work. This single sentence closes the entire military narrative of Joshua 1-11. Everything that follows — the tribal allotments of chapters 13-21 — flows from this rest. The phrase echoes YHWH's promise of "rest" (menukhah) in Deuteronomy 12:10 and Joshua 1:13. PNEUMA sets this final line apart from the preceding verse to give it the weight the narrator intended: a full stop on an era.

**On the Vaticanus LXX:** The Greek of Joshua 11 follows the Hebrew closely, with a few notable divergences. In v. 1, the LXX adds "the king of Marron" as an additional coalition member not found in the MT. In v. 8, Vaticanus reads "Maseron" for the MT's Misrephoth-maim. In v. 13, the LXX renders the Hebrew "their tel" with the participle kechomatismenas ("the mounded [cities]"), confirming the sense of cities built on accumulated ruins. In v. 21, the LXX sequence of cities differs slightly and includes "Kadesh" where the MT does not. The summary formula of v. 23 is essentially identical in both traditions. PNEUMA follows the Masoretic text of the Leningrad Codex as its primary source throughout.

— PNEUMA
